summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'infodecks' - slide decks designed purely for reading rather than presentation, distinguishing them from the problematic 'slideuments' that try to serve both purposes. He argues that infodecks have legitimate advantages: spatial layout aids explanation, they discourage unread long prose, and diagrams become primary communication elements. While acknowledging that infodecks are often dismissed as unserious by both casual users and professional writers, Fowler advocates taking them seriously as communication vehicles, especially as tablet computing becomes more prevalent. He shares his experiments with both PDF and HTML formats, ultimately preferring HTML for its dynamic capabilities despite tooling challenges.
key_points: Infodecks differ from slideuments because they serve a single purpose (reading) rather than trying to work for both presentation and reading
The rise of tablets and screen-based reading makes diagram-heavy, colorful documents more practical than paper-optimized prose
A 'lede-and-details' approach works well: large-font summary sentences let readers skim, with detailed text blocks available for depth
HTML offers advantages over PDF for infodecks (animation, web integration) but existing tools are designed for live presentations, not reading
Most presentation tools load entire decks into memory, which crashes memory-limited devices - a problem Fowler solved by building custom tooling
money_quotes: Slideuments are bad because they try to do two different things, but an infodeck is there only for reading, so can be done well.

Most users look at them as something they just knock up with PowerPoint, and writers with pretentions to seriousness (such as myself) consider infodecks to be beneath us. I'm beginning to think we should take them more seriously.
People like me can snark about managers these days being unable to read anything that doesn't look like a bullet point, but there are advantages to these infodecks.
Modern computers have bright multi-color screens, and aren't worried about conserving paper.
The idea is that the reader can get a good sense of what the deck says by skipping through the slides just reading the ledes.
I think of them as short paragraphs rather than bulleted sentences.
key_insight: Infodecks deserve serious attention as a communication form because they're optimized for a single purpose - reading on screens - rather than being compromised slideuments that fail at both presentation and documentation.
